content:
Obligation is an encounter card type representing a commitment or obstacle an identity's alter-ego might face. Abilities on obligations using 'you'/'your' apply only to the player whose play area it's in.

  • Each identity is associated with one or more obligation cards, shuffled into the encounter deck during setup if that identity is played. Identity-specific obligation cards are part of that identity-specific set and may only be used alongside that identity.
  • If an obligation is revealed and instructs it must be given to a specific player, place it in that player's play area (they decide resolution); if it cannot be given for any reason, ignore the ability, remove it from the game, and reveal an additional encounter card.
  • If revealed without a specific-player instruction, it goes to the revealing player's play area.
  • If drawn from a player deck, it goes to that player's play area (no replacement draw unless refilling to hand size).

Effects beginning with 'otherwise' resolve only if the preceding effect was not resolved — either because a condition was not true, or the effect could not at least partially resolve. If 'otherwise' is preceded by a semicolon, 'preceding effect' refers to effects before the semicolon in the same sentence; if its own sentence, refers to the directly preceding sentence.

• If an ally is defeated by an attack with overkill, deal any damage beyond its hit points to the identity of the player who controls the ally.
  • If a minion is defeated this way, deal excess damage to the villain. Equivalent ability: 'If this attack defeats an ally, deal the damage beyond its hit points to the identity of that ally's controller.
  • If this attack defeats a minion, deal the damage beyond its hit points to the villain.'
  • Damage dealt by overkill is considered damage from an attack, but doesn't constitute an attack against that character.
  • If excess damage from overkill is prevented, that damage is not dealt to the identity/villain.

• A card's owner is the player whose deck contained the card at the start of setup; the scenario owns the encounter deck and each encounter card. Identity cards are owned/controlled by the player playing as that identity.
  • Cards enter play under their owner's control; encounter cards are under the scenario's control. Upgrades attached to a card controlled by a different player are controlled by that other player.
  • When a player takes control of a campaign-specific or scenario-specific player card with a player card back, they become the owner until the game ends or another player takes control. Control remains constant unless an ability explicitly causes a change.
  • A player controls cards in their own out-of-play areas. Control changes remain in effect until: the changing ability ceases; the card leaves play; the card is a played event; or the card is discarded from hand — reverting to owner's equivalent area.
